headline: Regime Flips Risk-On: The RRP Drain Just Finished
body: The regime scores +2 and crosses into Risk-On for the first time since we started tracking. Full pilot positions are now allowed (3–5% starters). Net liquidity jumped to $5.897T, up +1.54% over the past 13 weeks — well above the +0.5% trigger.

The mechanical reason: overnight reverse repo (RRP) — a parking lot where money-market funds stash idle cash at the Fed overnight, where it sits outside markets — hit zero this week. $0.0T. For context, RRP peaked above $2.5T in 2023. That entire pool has now drained back into the financial system over two years. When RRP drains, those dollars don’t vanish — they flow into Treasury bills, credit markets, and eventually risk assets. The injection is complete. Net liquidity is now the Fed balance sheet ($6.725T) minus almost nothing in RRP, minus the Treasury’s own account ($0.828T TGA). Clean and high.

Credit and dollar are not yet confirming. High-yield credit spreads (HY OAS) sit at 2.8% — historically tight, basically no stress — but the 13-week change is only −0.29pp, just shy of the −0.3pp threshold for a bullish signal. The broad dollar index is +0.67% over 13 weeks, below the +1% warning level. Both are neutral, not headwinds. The yield curve (10y−2y) is back to +0.42%, positive and gently steepening — a quiet green flag.

On the GARP screen: 30 candidates passed the PEG ≤1.5 gate this week, 11 of them scoring 5/5 on all Lynch criteria. The standouts by numbers before looking at names: CAND-01 (Industrials, PEG 0.44, EPS +30%, ROE 39%) is unusually cheap for its growth quality — the kind of mid-cap compounder Lynch built his career on. CAND-02 and CAND-03 (both Information Technology, PEG 0.62 and 0.74, EPS +11%, ROE above 20%) are large-cap quality growing at a reasonable price. CAND-04 (Health Care, PEG 0.74, EPS +15%, ROE 14%) is a software-adjacent healthcare name. Worth a flag: CAND-12 and CAND-14 have eye-catching PEGs below 0.5 and EPS growth above 400% — these are semiconductor cycle rebounds, not durable Lynch-style growers. Score 4/5 because they fail the 10–40% EPS sweet-spot test. Use them as a cyclical call, not a compounder.
13F smart-money data is temporarily unavailable — SEC EDGAR is rate-throttling the local connection this week. Will update when it clears.
watch: TGA drawdown: the Treasury’s $0.828T checking account will be spent down as the debt ceiling is resolved — each dollar spent pushes more liquidity into markets. Watch for a drop below $0.5T as an additional tailwind.
Dollar threshold: the broad index is at 120.08, up +0.67% over 13 weeks. If it crosses +1%, the dollar signal flips bearish (tighter global liquidity). Not there yet but watch it weekly.
HY OAS confirmation: spreads at 2.8% are just −0.01pp from triggering a bullish credit signal. Any slight further tightening would push the regime score to +3, the strongest possible reading.

CAND-01 deep dive: the Industrials 5/5 name at PEG 0.44 with 30% EPS growth and 39% ROE is worth a Phase 3 Damodaran health check. At that valuation gap, the market either knows something or is wrong.
change_my_mind: HY OAS spiking above 4% would flip credit bearish and likely knock the regime back to Neutral regardless of liquidity. Credit is the fastest-moving signal.
Dollar breaking above +1% (index ~121.3) would add a bearish point. Combined with any liquidity softening, that could reverse the regime call within weeks.
Net liquidity falling on the 13-week trend — if the Fed accelerates QT or TGA refills sharply after a debt ceiling deal, the score drops. The RRP buffer is gone now, so there is no cushion left.
